title: const — the right default
instruction:
`const` is your default variable declaration. It signals that the binding won't change, which makes code easier to reason about and helps the compiler optimize.

Run this and observe how const variables behave.
successMessage: Three const declarations — TypeScript inferred `string`, `number`, and `boolean` without annotations. If you tried to reassign any of them, TypeScript would immediately error.

title: let — for values that change
instruction:
`let` is block-scoped and reassignable. Use it when a value needs to change — like a counter, a score, or a running total.

See how let tracks a changing score across multiple updates.
successMessage: `let` allows reassignment — `score` changed three times. TypeScript still enforces the type: once TypeScript infers `score` as `number`, you can't assign a string to it.

title: Type annotations — be explicit when needed
instruction:
While TypeScript infers types from values, sometimes you need to annotate explicitly — especially when declaring a variable without immediately assigning a value.

Declare variables with and without annotations.
successMessage: When you declare without assigning, TypeScript can't infer the type — so you must annotate. Try assigning `loginCount = "forty-two"` and TypeScript would immediately tell you that's wrong.

title: Inference vs annotation — when to use each
instruction:
A common question: when should you annotate, when should you let TypeScript infer?

Rule: annotate when the type isn't obvious from the value, or when you're declaring without assigning. Let TypeScript infer everything else.
successMessage: Inference keeps code clean for obvious cases. Function return type annotation `: number` tells callers what to expect without reading the implementation.

title: const with objects — binding vs mutation
instruction:
`const` prevents reassigning the variable itself, but the object it points to can still be mutated. This trips up many beginners.

See the difference between binding immutability and value immutability.
successMessage: `const user` means the variable `user` always points to the same object. It doesn't mean the object is frozen. Use `Object.freeze()` or `Readonly<T>` if you need deep immutability.

title: const with arrays — same rule applies
instruction:
Arrays declared with `const` can still be pushed to, sorted, and modified. `const` only prevents reassigning the variable.

Build a todo list with a const array.
successMessage: `const todos` prevents `todos = []` (new array assignment) but not `todos.push()`. The annotation `string[]` ensures only strings can be added — `todos.push(42)` would be a type error.

title: Destructuring with const
instruction:
Destructuring lets you unpack values from objects and arrays into named variables. Combined with `const`, it's one of the most readable patterns in TypeScript.

Destructure a user object and a coordinates array.
successMessage: Destructuring creates named variables from object properties or array positions. TypeScript infers each variable's type from its source — `name` is `string`, `lat` is `number`.

title: Default values in destructuring
instruction:
When destructuring, you can provide default values for missing properties. This is a clean pattern for config objects with optional fields.

Destructure a config object with sensible defaults.
successMessage: Default values in destructuring replace `undefined` (not `null`). The second call uses all defaults — a clean pattern for optional configuration.

title: Renaming during destructuring
instruction:
Destructuring lets you rename variables using `originalName: newName`. This is useful when the property name conflicts with an existing variable or when you want a more descriptive name.

Rename during destructuring to avoid naming conflicts.
successMessage: `name: productName` reads as "take `name` from the object and call the local variable `productName`". Useful when the API uses generic names like `name` or `id` that you want to make more specific.

title: Rest syntax in destructuring
instruction:
The `...rest` syntax captures all remaining properties or elements that weren't individually destructured. It's perfect for separating "known" fields from "everything else".

Separate a user's core identity from their metadata.
expectedOutput: Metadata keys: createdAt, updatedAt, lastLogin, xp, plan
successMessage: `...metadata` captures all remaining properties. This pattern is used when you want to split an object — e.g., separating the body of a user record from its audit fields.

title: Template literals — powerful string interpolation
instruction:
Template literals (backtick strings) enable multi-line strings and embedded expressions. They're far more readable than string concatenation.

Build a formatted report using template literals.
successMessage: Template literals can span multiple lines and embed any JavaScript expression inside `${}`. `.trim()` removes the leading/trailing newlines from the multiline string.

title: Nullish coalescing — safe defaults
instruction:
The `??` operator returns the right side when the left is `null` or `undefined` (but not `0` or `false`). This is safer than `||` for numeric/boolean defaults.

See the difference between `??` and `||` for default values.
successMessage: `??` treats `0` and `false` as real values (only `null`/`undefined` trigger the default). If you used `||`, Alice's `xp` of `0` would wrongly display as `0` via the fallback — both happen to be 0 here, but `false` for `isPremium` would become the fallback `false`, which is still correct but for the wrong reason.

title: Optional chaining — safe navigation
instruction:
Optional chaining `?.` short-circuits to `undefined` if the left side is `null` or `undefined`, instead of throwing. It's essential when working with data that may be partially populated.

Safely navigate a deeply nested object.
successMessage: `?.` prevents the dreaded `Cannot read properties of undefined`. Without it, `bob.address.city` would throw because `bob.address` is `undefined`. With `?.`, it safely returns `undefined` instead.

title: Type widening and narrowing with variables
instruction:
TypeScript widens types when you use `let` (the value might change) and narrows them when you use `const` (the value is fixed). This affects literal types.

See how `const` creates narrower types than `let`.
successMessage: `const direction = "north"` has type `"north"` — a literal type. This is why it's accepted as a `Direction` without a cast. `let status = "pending"` has type `string` — too wide to use as a `Direction`.
